segments.s125-g1: ازان پس (azān pas) is an adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' or 'thereupon.' به کاووس is the indirect object 'to Kavus' (به + proper name). گوینده گفت places the agent 'the speaker' before the verb گفت; here گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (classical/epic past), so the whole chunk = 'thereupon the speaker said to Kavus.'
segments.s125-g2: که introduces reported speech ('that'). او دختری دارد is a present/persistent possession construction: دارد (present of داشتن) = 'has/possesses' and دختری = 'a daughter' (indefinite). اندر نهفت is a prepositional + participial/adjectival phrase: اندر = 'in/within' and نهفت = 'hidden/concealed', so 'hidden within.'
segments.s125-g3: که introduces a relative/continuation of the reported description ('that/ who'). از ... زیباترست is a comparative: از = 'than' (poetic from/of), سرو = 'cypress' (a standard simile for tall beauty), بالاش functions as 'lofty/high' modifying سرو, and زیباترست is an archaic/predicative comparative meaning 'is/more beautiful.' So: 'who is more beautiful than the tall cypress.'
segments.s125-g4: ز here is the poetic form of از meaning 'made of' or 'of.' مشک سیه = 'black musk' (a material/ornament). بر سرش is the locative phrase 'upon her head' (بر + سر + possessive -ش). افسرست is a predicative nominal (افسر 'diadem/helmet' + ست 'is') meaning 'is a diadem' or 'a diadem sits (on her head).' The clause literally: 'a diadem of black musk is on her head.'
segments.s125-g5: به ... بلند and به گیسو کمند are two parallel predicative phrases joined by و. به (here) marks the quality or respect ('in/with regard to'); بالا بلند = 'high/tall in height' (emphatic doubling of arriba + adjective), and گیسو کمند = 'hair (گیسو) [is] a snare (کمند)' — a poetic simile: her hair is like a snare/lasso. The construction omits an explicit copula (is) as is normal in Persian poetry.
segments.s125-g6: زبانش چو خنجر لبانش چو قند uses two similes in parallel. زبانش = 'her tongue/speech' (possessive -ش) and چو خنجر = 'like a dagger' (chō = 'as/like'), implying sharp speech. لبانش = 'her lips' and چو قند = 'like sugar' (sweet). The pattern is noun+possessive + چو + simile-noun; again the copula is implicit.
segments.s125-g7: بهشتیست is a predicate meaning 'is paradisal/heavenly' (بهشتی + است). آراسته and پرنگار are coordinate adjectives meaning 'adorned' and 'richly painted/ornamented'; they stack after the predicative be-phrase as descriptive epithets. The overall effect is a series of adjectival qualities ascribed to the subject.
segments.s125-g8: چو خورشید تابان = 'like the shining sun' (چو = 'as/like', خورشید = 'sun', تابان = 'shining'). به خرم بهار is an adverbial/temporal phrase 'in/at the joyful spring' (به used poetically for 'in' or 'during'), completing the simile: 'like the shining sun in the joyous spring.'
segments.s125-g33: بشد is a past narrative verb here meaning 'there came' / 'he came' (it became/it happened). The following nominal phrase مرد بیدار روشن‌روان is the subject: مرد (man/person) modified by the adjectives بیدار (alert) and روشن‌روان (bright‑souled), placed after the noun in classical poetic order.
segments.s125-g34: به نزدیک is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'toward / near'; سالار هاماوران is the addressee/target (the chief/lord of Hamavaran). Word order places the motion/approach phrase after the subject.
segments.s125-g35: زبان کرد is a light verb construction where the noun 'زبان' (speech) combines with کرد ('did/made') to mean 'he spoke / made his speech'. گویا here means 'eloquent' (a predicative adjective). The conjunction و links the second parallel clause دل کرد گرم, where دل ('heart') + کرد + گرم means 'his heart became warm / he was emotionally stirred.' The verbs are simple narrative past forms.
segments.s125-g36: بیاراست is a past transitive verb 'he adorned / he set'; لب را is the definite direct object (را marks it). به گفتار نرم is an instrument/manner phrase 'with/into gentle speech' (به here = 'by/with'). The clause means literally 'he adorned the lip with gentle speech' → 'he set his lips to soft words.'
segments.s125-g37: ز here means 'from' (indicating source). دادش is the past verb داد ('gave') with the enclitic pronoun ش = 'to him' ('gave him'); فروان qualifies سلام as 'abundant/many' — together 'Kavus gave him plentiful greetings' (or 'he received plentiful greetings from Kavus').
segments.s125-g38: ازان پس is an adverbial phrase 'thereupon / after that'. بگفت is the past narrative 'he said / he told'. آنچ is a relative/indefinite 'that which', followed by بود (past of 'to be') and از پیام ('concerning the message'), so the clause means 'he told what the message was.'
segments.s125-g39: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' and ازو indicates the source 'of it / from him'. شاه هاماوران is the subject 'the king of Hamavaran' — classical word order allows the verb and its object/source before the subject.
segments.s125-g40: دلش (دل + enclitic ـش = 'his heart') گشت پر درد uses گشت 'became' with پر (here 'full (of)') and درد 'pain/sorrow' to mean 'his heart became full of sorrow'. The coordinate و links سر شد گران, where سر 'head' + شد 'became' + گران 'heavy' expresses physical/mental burden — a parallel clause describing his distress.
segments.s125-g9: نشاید is an impersonal/optative-style negative meaning “it is not fitting” or “ought not to be.” که introduces a subordinate clause (’that…’) and باشد is the subjunctive/present-form of ‘to be’ (here: “that there should be”). به جز is a compound prepositional phrase meaning “except / besides,” and جفت شاه is a noun phrase ‘a mate/consort for the king’ (noun + possessor), so the whole segment means “it is not fitting that there should be, except, a mate for the king.”
segments.s125-g10: چه نیکو is an exclamatory phrase: چه (how/what a) + نیکو (good/beautiful), and بود is the past copula ‘was’ (here expressing the wished/imagined quality). شاه را uses را as a topical/definite marker indicating that the king is the one affected, and جفت ماه is the noun phrase ‘a pair of moons’ (ماه used poetically for beautiful ones). Together: “How beautiful would a pair of moons be for the king.”
segments.s125-g11: بجنبید here describes a motion/stirring (in classical poetic usage it functions as a past/resultative: ‘was moved/was stirred’). کاووس را places Kavus as the affected person with را marking him as topic/accusative, دل is the thing that moved (‘heart’) and ز جای literally ‘from its place’ (ز = from, جای = place). Word order is poetic but the meaning is ‘Kavus’s heart was moved from its place.’
segments.s125-g12: چنین داد پاسخ is a fixed combination: چنین ‘thus’ + داد ‘gave’ + پاسخ ‘answer’ = ‘thus gave answer.’ که introduces the content clause, اینست is a present copula ‘this is,’ and رای means ‘opinion/counsel/decision.’ So the clause reports: ‘thus he answered that “this is the counsel.”’
segments.s125-g13: گزین کرد is a compound verbal phrase where گزین (choose/select) + کرد (do/make) together form the past tense ‘chose.’ شاه is the agent/topic ‘the king’ (word order is inverted for poetic effect). از میان گروه is the prepositional phrase ‘from among the group/company.’
segments.s125-g14: یکی here means ‘one / a certain,’ followed by مرد ‘man.’ بیدار is an adjective ‘awake/alert,’ and دانش‌پژوه is a compound noun ‘scholar/student.’ Together they form an appositive: ‘one man, an alert scholar.’
segments.s125-g15: This segment strings descriptive adjectives: گرانمایه ‘precious/esteemed,’ و ‘and,’ گرد in this context is an adjectival or descriptive word (poetic; here linking qualities — see note) and و separates it from the next clause; مغزش is مغز ‘brain/mind’ with the possessive suffix -ش ‘his,’ and گران is an adjective meaning ‘valuable/heavy/esteemed.’ The phrase attributes high value and a weighty/valuable mind to the person chosen. (Note: گرد is poetic and joins qualities; here rendered as part of the list of attributes.)
segments.s125-g16: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning ‘he commanded/ordered.’ تا here functions as ‘so that / in order that,’ introducing the result clause شد ‘became’ (or ‘was made’), به is the preposition ‘to/into,’ and هاماوران (a proper/place or collective name in the poem) is the destination/office — so the whole segment means ‘he ordered that he be made/placed into Hamavaran.’
segments.s125-g97: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral/introducer 'one'/'a certain' placed before the noun; انجمن is the noun 'assembly/meeting'. ساخت is a past‑tense transitive verb 'made/formed/held' (3rd person sing.) and takes the following از‑phrase as its complement (i.e. 'made/held an assembly composed of ...'). از introduces the constituents of that assembly ('out of/from'), and بخردان is a genitive/partitive plural noun phrase 'the wise (ones)'.
segments.s125-g98: ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'of/from' and here introduces an appositive/member of the assembly. بیداردل is a compound adjective 'wakeful‑hearted/vigilant' modifying پیر 'old/elder' (here an elder person). سر موبدان is a nominal phrase 'the head/chief of the mobeds' (سر = chief/leader, موبدان = mōbed priests). Grammatically the whole phrase specifies one of the wise who composed the assembly.
segments.s125-g99: سزا دید is an idiomatic verbal phrase meaning 'it was judged/deserved' or 'they deemed it proper'; دید is the past verb 'saw/considered'. سودابه را is the definite direct object (را marks the object). جفت خویش is a noun phrase meaning 'her own mate/spouse' — خوی (kin/own) plus the enclitic possessive ش 'her' — and functions as the complement/target with respect to the ensuing verb (i.e. what the decision concerns).
segments.s125-g100: ببستند is a past‑plural verb 'they bound/tied/concluded' (here 'they bound/made bound'). عهدی is the direct object 'a covenant/pledge' and بر introduces the domain/terms: بر آیین و کیش = 'according to/in the terms of ritual and creed' (آیین = ritual/custom, کیش = creed/sect). Word order places objects before the verb (common in Persian): سودابه را and عهدی are linked as object and object of the binding action, while بر آیین و کیش is an adverbial/relational phrase specifying the form or basis of the covenant.
segments.s125-g17: چنین گفت is a short phrase 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus' precedes the verb). رایش = 'his counsel/opinion' (object of the following imperative). به من = indirect object 'for/to me'. تازه کن is an imperative construction where تازه (literally 'fresh/new') combines with کن (imperative of کردن 'do') to mean 'make fresh' or 'renew'. The whole segment is a reported imperative: 'Renew his counsel for me.'
segments.s125-g18: بیارای is an imperative verb meaning 'adorn/beautify' (poetic imperative form). مغزش is مغز + enclitic ـش 'his brain/mind' and functions as the direct object. به شیرین سخن gives manner/medium: به + noun phrase 'in/with sweet speech' (شیرین modifies سخن). Together this continues the previous command: 'Adorn his mind with sweet words.'
segments.s125-g19: بگویش is the imperative 'tell him' (بگو + enclitic ش 'to him'). که is the complementizer 'that' introducing reported content. پیوند ما is the noun phrase 'our bond/alliance' and در جهان places it locatively 'in the world.' This segment sets up the clause being reported: 'Tell him that our alliance, in the world ...'.
segments.s125-g20: بجویند is a 3rd-person plural subjunctive/optative ('they seek' / 'will seek') used inside reported speech after که. کار آزموده is a compound attributive phrase: کار 'deed/affair' + آزموده 'tried/experienced', together 'experienced in affairs' modifying مهان 'guests' (the subject). The clause means 'experienced guests will seek (it).' The subject (مهان) follows the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s125-g21: که here introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'). خورشید روشن = 'the shining/bright sun' (noun + adjective). ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of'. تاج منست is a compact predication in which منست functions as a poetic copula/predicate relating تاج to the speaker ('is of me' / 'belongs to me' / 'is my crown'). The segment states that the sun's brightness is associated with the speaker's crown.
segments.s125-g22: زمین is the subject 'the earth.' پایه تخت عاج is a noun phrase meaning 'the pedestal (پایه) of the ivory (عاج) throne (تخت)' with the modifiers placed in compact poetic order. منست again functions as the copula/predicative element linking that phrase to the speaker's possession/status ('is [my] ...'). The whole segment asserts that the earth serves as the base of the speaker's ivory throne.
segments.s125-g23: هرانکس که is a correlative 'whoever' + relative marker introducing the condition. در سایه من پناه is a noun phrase 'refuge in my shadow' where در سایه من = 'in my shade/protection' and پناه 'refuge' is the object that will (not) be found in the next segment. Grammatically this segment provides the protasis (if-clause) without its verb so the verb appears in the following segment.
segments.s125-g24: نیابد is a negative-form verb meaning 'does not find' / 'fails to obtain' (3rd-person singular subjunctive/indicative form used in conditional sense). ازو = 'from him/it' (referring back to the shadow/possessor). کم شود پایگاه is a predicate 'the footing/base will become less' (کم 'less' + شود 'become' + پایگاه 'standing/base'); together: 'his standing will be diminished.' The whole gives the apodosis: if he finds no refuge in my shadow, his base/standing will lessen.
segments.s125-g25: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now' placed at the start for emphasis. با تو پیوند جویم is a verb phrase in which پیوند (a noun 'bond') is the object and جویم (first‑person present/subjunctive form of جستن) means 'I seek' or 'I seek to make'; the pronoun تو follows the preposition با ('with you'). همی is a poetic particle reinforcing the ongoing or present action.
segments.s125-g26: رخ آشتی را is a definite object (را marks رخِ آشتی 'the face of reconciliation'). بشویم is an older/poetic present/subjunctive of شدن here meaning 'I become' (so: 'I shall be/ I will become'); grammatically the speaker says he will 'become the face of reconciliation.' همی again is a present-tense/emphatic particle.
segments.s125-g27: پس at the start gives a consecutive/contrastive 'then' or 'moreover.' پرده تو ('your curtain/veil') places the possessor after the noun (noun + pronoun). یکی دخترست is a verbless nominal clause meaning 'is a certain/a single maiden' (یکی here = 'a certain' or 'one'), with -ست appended as the archaic copula 'is'.
segments.s125-g28: شنیدم که introduces a reported clause ('I heard that ...'). گاه is a temporal adverb 'at times.' مرا درخورست is the predicate: مرا is the beneficiary/object 'for me' (here written in the segment list as م, an enclitic pronoun), and درخورست is the copular + adjective meaning 'is fitting/appropriate.' Together: 'I have heard that at times she is fitting for me.'
segments.s125-g29: که introduces explanatory content ('for' / 'that'). پاکیزه تخم‌ست is a nominal clause: پاکیزه qualifies تخم (here 'lineage'/'seed'), and تخم‌ست ('is of seed/origin') is an archaic way to say 'is of pure birth/origin.' و پاکیزه تن repeats the adjective with تن ('body'), i.e. 'and of pure body.'
segments.s125-g30: ستوده is a past participle used predicatively 'praised/celebrated.' به هر شهر و هر انجمن uses به + locative/regular prepositional phrase 'in/through every city and every assembly' (here به is functioning as 'in' or 'throughout'), with هر … و هر … enumerating locations where she is famed.
segments.s125-g31: چو at the start of the clause is conditional 'if' (here introducing a protasis). داماد یابی is a second‑person form meaning 'you find' (یابی is subjunctive/optative/second‑person verbal form used in conditional contexts). The following چو پور قباد uses چو as 'like/as' and پور قباد specifies 'the son of Qobad' as the model for the match.
segments.s125-g32: چنان دان is an imperative/optative instruction 'consider/think thus' (دان = 'consider'). که introduces the content of that consideration: خورشید داد تو داد is a compact poetic clause meaning roughly 'the sun has given (him) to you' — خورشید is the subject 'the sun,' داد here is a verb 'gave,' تو 'to you,' and the final داد repeats or strengthens the idea of giving (poetic repetition).
segments.s125-g89: This is a compact nominal phrase: یکی ('a certain') precedes the noun to single out the host. لشکر (host/army) is the head noun and آراسته (adorned) is a past-participial adjective placed after the noun, a normal order in Persian poetry. چو + بهشت makes a simile ('like paradise'), with چو introducing the comparison before its complement.
segments.s125-g90: تو گفتی is the past-tense (perfect) address 'you said'; که introduces a reported/content clause. روی زمین is a prepositional phrase ('on the surface of the earth') where روی (here = 'on/upon') governs زمین. لاله کشت is a verbless verblike nominal phrase common in poetry: کشت (literally 'planting'/'crop') with لاله as its specifier gives 'a planting of tulips' or 'the earth was sown with tulips'—the predicate meaning is supplied by the subordinate clause introduced by که.
segments.s125-g91: چو آمد is a temporal/subordinating clause head: چو here = 'when', آمد is past 'came' (he came). به نزدیک is a prepositional phrase 'near to' (به + نزدیک), and کاووس شاه follows as the subject 'King Kavus' (name + title). Word order places the time/clause before the main clause, as is common.
segments.s125-g92: This is the main clause describing the king's inner state: دل آرام = 'heart (was) calm' (دل 'heart' + adjective آرام 'calm'); Persian often omits a copula in such predicative adjective constructions. با introduces accompanying qualities ('with beauty') and is repeated before فر to stack characteristics; زیب, فر and جاه are nouns in a asyndetic series linked by و, listing the attributes that fill his heart.
segments.s125-g93: This is a parallel enumerative phrase: دو + noun gives 'two [of]' (دو یاقوت = 'two rubies'), followed by the adjective خندان 'smiling'. The next pair repeats the pattern (دو نرگس = 'two narcissi') with دژم 'sullen/grim' qualifying them. The sequence lists features (metaphorical descriptions of the face) in balanced pairs.
segments.s125-g94: ستون دو ابرو is a metaphorical noun phrase: ستون 'pillar/column' followed by a genitive-like phrase دو ابرو 'two eyebrows' — literally 'pillars, the two eyebrows'. چو introduces a simile 'like', and سیمین قلم is a two-word simile 'silver (simin) pen (qalam)', with قلم used image‑wise (pen/stylus). The overall construction compares the eyebrows to silver pens/columns.
segments.s125-g95: نگه کرد is a light-verb construction: نگه 'a look/gaze' + کرد (past of کردن 'to do') = 'he looked'. کاووس is the subject. و joins the next verb; خیره بماند is a compound where خیره 'staring/astonished' is predicative and بماند (present subjunctive/imperative form of ماندن used here as past narrative) means 'he remained/was left'—together 'he stood staring/was left aghast'.
segments.s125-g96: به سودابه is the indirect object 'to Sudabeh' (به + proper name). بر here functions as a preposition 'upon/with/regarding' leading into the accusative phrase 'بر نام یزدان' ('in the name of God' / 'invoking God's name'), where نام یزدان = 'the name of God'. بخواند is a present-subjunctive/epic-imperative/formal past of خواندن used narratively as 'he called/summoned' or 'he cried (out)'; together the phrase means 'he called to Sudabeh, invoking the name of God.'
segments.s125-g49: 'چنین' is an adverb meaning 'thus/so'; 'گفت' is the 3rd person singular past tense 'he said' (poetic narration). 'با' marks the addressee 'to/with', and 'مرد شیرین سخن' is a noun phrase 'the man of sweet speech' where 'شیرین' (sweet) modifies 'سخن' (speech) to mean 'sweet‑spoken man'. Word order is Subject-Verb + prepositional phrase for the listener.
segments.s125-g50: 'که' introduces a subordinate clause 'that/which'; 'سر نیست' is an existential/negational phrase 'there is no head/origin'; 'این آرزو را' (object marked by 'را') topicalizes 'this desire' as the thing lacking a head; the final 'نه بن' is a brief negative addition 'nor (has it) a root/foundation' (بن = root/foundation). The clause is somewhat elliptical/poetic but means the desire has neither head nor foundation.
segments.s125-g51: 'همی' is an emphatic/continuous adverb (classical usage) giving a sense of ongoing or habitual action; 'خواهد' is 3rd person present/factual 'he wants/asks'; 'از من' marks source 'from me'; 'گرامی دو چیز' is the direct object 'two honored/precious things' (adjective + numeral + noun). The verb precedes the object here, a normal narrative order in older Persian.
segments.s125-g52: 'که' introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'which/that'; 'آن را' is the demonstrative object 'that/it' with object marker 'را' (referring back to the two things); 'سه دیگر' is the agent/subject 'three others'; 'ندانیم' is 1st person plural present 'we do not know' (classical form), and 'نیز' adds 'also/likewise'. Word order places the object pronoun before the subject for topical emphasis.
segments.s125-g53: 'مرا' (appearing here split as 'م' + 'را' in the tokenization) marks the experiencer/indirect object 'to me'; 'پشت‌گرمی' means 'reassurance/solace'; 'بد' qualifies it as 'bad/ill' (i.e. false or inadequate consolation); 'از خواسته' specifies the source 'from the desire/wish'. The whole phrase = 'to me a bad consolation from that desire.'
segments.s125-g54: 'به فرزند' = 'for/with respect to a child/son' (prepositional phrase marking the object of hope); 'بودم' is 1st person singular past 'I was/had been'; 'دل آراسته' is a predicate phrase 'heart adorned/prepared'. The order is somewhat poetic/inverted (verb before the predicate), giving 'I had my heart prepared for a son.'
segments.s125-g55: 'به من' is dative 'to/for me'; 'زین سپس' (poetic variant of 'از این پس') = 'from this time onward'; 'جان نماند' = 'life did not remain' (نماند is past negative 'did not remain/left'); and the final 'همی' gives an emphatic/continuous nuance. Together: 'From that time onward life ceased to remain for me.'
segments.s125-g56: 'وگر' = 'and/or if' introducing a hypothetical; 'شاه ایران' is the subject 'the Shah/King of Iran'; 'ستاند' here is an archaic/classical verb meaning 'to take/snatch (away)' (poetic usage), so the clause reads 'if the Shah of Iran were to take it'; final 'همی' again adds the classical emphatic or modal flavor. The clause is conditional and somewhat elliptical, trailing into what follows in the next line.
segments.s125-g81: This is a prepositional phrase headed by به (to/for) marking the appointment/assignment: به + یک هفته سالار هاماوران = 'to/for one haftesālār of Hamāvarān'. هفته here is the numeral 'seven' used in the compound title (هفته‌سالار = commander/leader of seven), سالار is the title 'commander/chief', and هاماوران is a place-name/ethnic/place-term; the whole phrase names the office/person appointed.
segments.s125-g82: همی ساخت is a poetic past/continuousive construction meaning 'he made/established' or 'he caused to be made' (همی as an older continuous/habitual marker + ساخت past of ساختن). آن کار 'that work/affair' is the direct object, and با مهتران 'with the chiefs/notables' marks accompaniment — in other words, 'he did/made that arrangement with the chiefs.'
segments.s125-g83: بیاورد is past tense 'he brought' (used here as a narrative past). پس functions adverbially 'then/afterwards'. خسرو is the proper name/title (Khosrow) and خسته‌دل is an adjectival epithet 'heart‑weary' (postpositive in Persian, describing Khosrow).
segments.s125-g84: This segment gives what he brought. پرستنده here functions as a noun 'attendant/devoted follower' (a group of people accompanying him). The following number phrase سیصد ... چهل supplies the quantity 'three hundred and forty' (the word عماری in the manuscript/lexicon list does not transparently match a clear modern sense in this context, so its lexical function here is uncertain and likely part of the numeric/collective expression).
segments.s125-g85: This is a catalog of beasts in parallel: هزار ... و ... و ... هزار lists quantities and types. The individual nouns name animal types (here used as counted items), so the phrase reads 'a thousand [established term for female beast/mare?], and horses (اسپ), and a thousand camels (اشتر)'. The exact lexical entry for استر/اشتر in the provided senses is not a direct match, so those are treated as lexical items naming mounts/camels in context.
segments.s125-g86: ز is the poetic genitive/partitive 'of/from' introducing the material; دیبا 'brocade' and دینار 'dinars' are the goods. کردند is the past plural 'they made/did' and بار is 'load/cargo' — together 'they made/packed a load of brocade and dinars' (i.e., they filled the cargo with brocade and coins).
segments.s125-g87: عماری is a noun 'a dwelling/building' (here the object being described). به ماه نو 'at/by the new moon' is a time/likeness phrase (ماه نو = 'new moon' or 'new month') and آراسته is the perfect/adjectival participle 'adorned' — together: 'a dwelling adorned at/by the new moon' or 'a dwelling adorned like the new moon.'
segments.s125-g88: پس is 'then/afterwards'. پشت و پیش is a fixed pair meaning 'back and front' or 'behind and before' (i.e., all around); اندرون is 'the inner part/interior', and خواسته is the past participle/adjectival/verb form 'desired/wished for' — so the clause says he sought/wanted the inner quarters, both front and back.
segments.s125-g41: همی گفت is a literary past-continuous phrase: همی (an adverb) + گفت gives the sense 'he kept saying / he was saying'. هرچند is the concessive 'although'. کاو is an archaic compressed form (کـه + او) meaning 'that he'; پادشاست is a nominal predicate (پادشاه + ـست) meaning 'is a king' (copula fused to the noun). Word order is topical/concessive: 'He kept saying, although that he is king...'.
segments.s125-g42: A string of epithets linked by و: جهاندار (sovereign) و پیروز (victorious) و فرمان + روا + ست. Here فرمان + روا + ست functions as a compound predicate: فرمان (command) + روا (part of a compound implying rulership) + ست (copula 'is'). The overall role is appositive description of پادشاه ('he is a king, i.e. a sovereign, victorious and ruler').
segments.s125-g43: مرا is a topical/possessive object ('for me' / 'as for me') followed by the locative در جهان ('in the world') and pointing phrase این یکی ('this one'); دخترست (دختر + ـست) is the copular predicate 'is a daughter'. The literal order is topical: 'To me, in the world, this one is a daughter.'
segments.s125-g44: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. از before a noun here marks the standard of comparison ('than' in English) with the comparative گرامی‌ترست ('is dearer'). جان شیرین is the noun phrase 'sweet life' or 'dear life' that is being compared. The verb-ending ـست is the copula in comparative form: 'is more dear than...'.
segments.s125-g45: فرستاده + را is the definite object (the envoy). گر (here merged with را in the data as را‌گر) is the conditional 'if'. کنم is 1st-person present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/do' used in the protasis: 'if I make/treat (him) ...'. سرد و خوار are two coordinated adjectives meaning 'cold/aloof and humiliated/despised'. The conditional structure is 'if I make the envoy cold and despised...'.
segments.s125-g46: ندارم is the negative present first-person 'I do not have'. پی و مایه are two nouns joined by و: پی here refers to 'followers/after/continuation' or 'pursuit' in the sense of backing or follow-up, and مایه is 'means/resources'. کارزار is the noun 'battle/war' governing both nouns: 'I have no followers and no resources for battle.'
segments.s125-g47: همان به که is a fixed colloquial/literary idiom meaning 'it is better that' or 'best that'. این درد را is the direct object 'this pain/sorrow' (را marks it definite). نیز 'also' adds that this action applies to this pain as well. چشم here is the noun 'eye' used in the idiom چشم بپوشیدن 'to turn a blind eye / to overlook'.
segments.s125-g48: بپوشم is the present-subjunctive of پوشیدن used in the idiom 'چشم بپوشم' meaning 'I should overlook/cover with my eye' (i.e., 'turn a blind eye'). و links the second clause. بر دل ('upon the heart') gives the location; بخوابیم here is jussive/subjunctive 'let ... sleep' (verb precedes its logical subject as a poetic inversion), and خشم (anger) is the subject that is to 'sleep' on the heart: 'let anger sleep on the heart' = 'let anger lie dormant'.
segments.s125-g65: همی + خواهد places the desire in a habitual/continuous aspect: همی here means “continually/always” and modifies خواهد (“wants/wishes”). از من is a simple prepositional object “from me.” که introduces a subordinate clause (that/that which). بی‌کام من is an adjectival phrase ‘my being unsatisfied’ (بی‌کام = unfulfilled), with من as the possessor/object.
segments.s125-g66: ببرد is the 3rd-person singular form of بردن used to express the wished-for action (optative/subjunctive sense: ‘may/might take away’ or simply ‘takes away’ in narrative). The sequence دل و خواب و آرام من lists three objects (heart, sleep, peace) with و as the coordinating conjunction; من repeats the possessor (‘my’). Word order is verb + objects which is common in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s125-g67: چه گویی تو اکنون is a direct question: چه ‘what’ + گویی (here functioning as ‘you say’ — an archaic/poetic present form) + تو ‘you’ + اکنون ‘now’. هوای تو چیست is a copular question ‘what is your desire/mood?’: هوای = desire/whim, تو = your, چیـست = is what (چی + ست).
segments.s125-g68: بدین کار is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘about/for this matter’ (بدین = به + این, in/with this; کار = matter/affair). بیدار رای تو چیست is another copular question: بیدار here qualifies رای (‘wakeful, deliberate’ opinion/plan) and رای تو = ‘your counsel/opinion’; چیـست again is the copula ‘is what’. The order is: locative/phrase + noun phrase + copula.
segments.s125-g69: بدو گفت سودابه is an inverted, somewhat archaic word order for ‘سودابه به او گفت’ — ‘Sudabeh said to him’. زین چاره نیست is a short clause: زین (from this / for this) + چاره ‘remedy/solution’ + نیست ‘is not/there is none’ — thus ‘there is no remedy for this.’
segments.s125-g70: ازو بهتر ... نیست is a comparative existential pattern: ازو بهتر literally ‘better than him’ followed by a predicate that is negated by نیست, giving ‘there is no one better than him.’ امروز is a temporal adverb ‘today.’ غمخواره is a noun meaning ‘mourner’/‘one given to grief’, so the clause says there is presently no one a better mourner than he.
segments.s125-g71: کسی کاو بود شهریار جهان is an archaic relative clause: کسی = ‘someone/anyone’, کاو here functions as an older fused form meaning ‘که او’ (‘who’/‘whoever’), بود = ‘was’ (past of بودن), شهریار جهان = ‘king/ruler of the world.’ The order is relative + verb + predicate (typical in classical Persian).
segments.s125-g72: بروبوم is a compound/poetic form meaning ‘over the land/realm’ (بَر بوم). خواهد همی is the habitual/future-like desire (‘would/will always want’ — همی marking continuative aspect, خواهد = wants/will). از مهان = ‘from guests/visitors’ marks the source/target of that desire. Overall the clause states a general truth about such rulers.
segments.s125-g57: سپارم is a first‑person verb form from سپردن meaning “I entrust / I leave (behind)” and here expresses the speaker’s action toward what follows. کنون is an adverb “now” placed after the verb (permissible in poetic word order). هرچ + خواهد forms a subordinate free‑relative “whatever he wills/wishes” (هرچ = “whatever”, خواهد = 3rd‑person modal/volitional “he will/wishes”). The final element بدوی is an archaic/contracted form (see senses) attached to that clause and completes the idea “whatever he wishes to (do)” so the whole segment means “I now leave whatever he wishes.”
segments.s125-g58: نتابم is a negative present/ability verb meaning “I do not endure / I will not submit” (here: “I will not bow/submit”). سر is the noun “head” and functions as the direct object of the following prepositional phrase. از marks separation/agentive source (“from / to” in the sense of ‘with respect to’), رای و فرمان is a coordinated noun phrase “counsel and command,” and اوی is an archaic possessive pronoun “his.” Together: “I will not lower my head to his counsel and command.”
segments.s125-g59: غمی گشت is a simple two‑word clause where غمی (a noun ‘grief’) + گشت (past of گشتن ‘became’) yields “a sorrow arose / he became sorrowful.” و links the clauses. سودابه را uses the object marker را to mark Sudabeh as the direct object, پیش here means “in the presence of / before [him],” and خواند (here) is the verb “he called / summoned.” Word order is subject/verb then object with را, standard for Persian but slightly compressed in poetry.
segments.s125-g60: ز is the poetic preposition often glossed “from / concerning” and introduces the topic (کاووس, Kavus). با او is the prepositional phrase “with her,” سخنها is the plural object “words / speeches,” and براند is an archaic 3rd‑person verb form (from راندن) used here to mean approximately “he put forth / uttered / addressed” — so the clause means “he addressed words to her about Kavus.” The clause follows the summons and describes what he then did.
segments.s125-g61: بدو is the dative/enclitic phrase “to him/to her” (here: “to her”). گفت is the reporting verb “said / told” introducing the following content clause (the listed lexical senses are nominal but the function here is the past‑tense verb). کز is the relative/introductory particle “that / who,” and مهتر سرفراز is the noun phrase “a distinguished / chief, proud and honored” describing the person being spoken about. The structure is ‘(He) said to her that [such a person].’
segments.s125-g62: که again introduces the subordinate (relative) clause “who/that.” هست is the copula “is.” از before مهی و بهی is a poetic use meaning roughly “in relation to / compared with the moon (مهی) and the beautiful/excellent (بهی)” — here it conveys the sense of surpassing or being independent of them. بی‌نیاز is the predicative adjective “self‑sufficient / without need.” The clause gives a quality of the ‘مهتر سرفراز’ just mentioned.
segments.s125-g63: فرستاده‌ای is an indefinite nominal form (a dispatched one / an envoy) and here functions as the subject “an envoy.” چرپ‌گوی is an epithet meaning “flatterer / flattering in speech” modifying فرستاده. آمدست is an archaic perfect construction (آمد + است fused) meaning “has come / has arrived.” The whole short clause announces the arrival: “A flattering envoy has come.”
segments.s125-g64: یکی نامه is an indefinite noun phrase “a certain letter” (یکی before the noun = ‘one / a certain’). چون here is the conjunction “as / when,” introducing a small clause describing the messenger’s action. زند is an archaic 3rd‑person verb form (here from زدن, ‘hits/puts/strikes/fastens/places’) used in context to mean something like “he lays/puts” (the lexicon given doesn’t include this verbal sense, hence ambiguity). و استا is “and stood / stopped” (استا used as a verbal/participial form), and به دست is the prepositional phrase “into/at the hand.” The phrase describes the messenger’s holding/placing of the letter in his hand.
segments.s125-g73: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/ because of' introducing the cause. پیوند با او is a nominal phrase 'the bond/connection with him' and چرایی دژم is a nominal predicate 'a reason for gloom' (چرایی = 'reason', دژم = 'sullen/grim'); the clause is effectively verbless/nominal and states the cause/background for what follows.
segments.s125-g74: کسی نشمرد is a negative past construction where نشمرد (past of شمردن) here means 'consider'/'reckon' and with کسی (in this position) yields 'no one' as subject. شادمانی به غم uses به in the sense 'as/into' (regard X as Y): 'regarded joy as sorrow.' Word order places the object/phrase before the verb, typical of classical poetic style.
segments.s125-g75: بدانست is a past-tense verb meaning 'realized' or 'perceived.' The subject سالار هاماوران follows the verb (a normal order in classical Persian poetry): 'the commander/leader of Hamavaran realized...'.
segments.s125-g76: که introduces the subordinate content clause. سودابه را uses را as the definite/object/experiencer marker (here marking Sudabeh as the person to whom the subsequent state applies). آن is anaphoric 'that (matter)', نیامد here is the idiomatic past negative sense 'did not seem / was not felt', and گران is a predicate adjective 'heavy/burdensome' — so: 'that (it) did not seem burdensome to Sudabeh.'
segments.s125-g77: فرستاده شاه را is the definite object 'the king's envoy' marked by را, and پیش 'before/in the presence of' indicates location; خواند (past of خواندن) here means 'called/summoned.' The clause therefore reads 'he summoned the king's messenger before him.'
segments.s125-g78: وزان is a connective 'and/from that,' linking to what follows. نامدارانش 'his notables' is the object, برتر ('foremost' / 'in front') modifies the seating, and نشاند is the archaic past 'caused to sit / seated (them)'. So the clause means he placed/seated his chief men in the foremost positions.
segments.s125-g79: ببستند is past plural 'they concluded/made,' and بندی is the object 'a binding / compact' (the verbal noun); together 'ببستند بندی' = 'they concluded a pact.' بر آیین خویش is a prepositional phrase 'according to their custom,' where خویش is the reflexive/possessive element (written here as خوی + ش = 'their own'). The object-before-verb order is typical in poetic diction.
segments.s125-g80: بران سان is a fixed expression 'in that manner / in such a way.' که introduces the relative/result clause; بود is the past copula 'was,' آن زمان is the temporal phrase 'that time' (i.e. 'at that time/then'), and دین خویش ('their religion/law') uses خویش (خوی + ش) as 'their own.' The clause therefore says the compact was such that, in that era, their religion/way remained theirs.

sentences.s125-s1: After that the speaker said to Kavus that he has a daughter concealed within.
sentences.s125-s2: That she was more beautiful than the lofty cypress; a diadem of black musk was on her head.
sentences.s125-s3: Tall in stature and with hair like a snare; her tongue like a dagger, her lips like sugar.
sentences.s125-s4: Paradisal, adorned and richly painted, like the shining sun in the joyous spring.
sentences.s125-s17: A vigilant, bright‑souled man came up to the commander of Hamavaran.
sentences.s125-s18: He spoke eloquently and his heart grew warm; he set his lips to gentle speech.
sentences.s125-s19: From Kavus he received abundant greetings; thereupon he told what the message was.
sentences.s125-s20: When the king of Hamavaran heard of it, his heart became full of sorrow and his head grew heavy.
sentences.s125-s5: It is not fitting that there should be any mate for the king besides; how fine would a pair of moons be for the king.
sentences.s125-s6: Kavus’s heart was stirred from its place; thus he gave answer: “This is the counsel.”
sentences.s125-s7: The king chose from among the company one man, an alert scholar.
sentences.s125-s8: Highly valued and honored, and with a precious mind, he was appointed by order to Hamavaran.
sentences.s125-s49: He convened an assembly of the wise: the wakeful‑hearted elder, the chief of the mobeds.
sentences.s125-s50: They judged it fitting that Sudabeh should be bound by a pledge to her mate, a covenant according to custom and creed.
sentences.s125-s9: Thus he said: “Renew his counsel for me; adorn his mind with sweet speech.”
sentences.s125-s10: Tell him that, in the world, experienced guests will seek our alliance.
sentences.s125-s11: For the bright sun is of my crown, and the earth is the pedestal of my ivory throne.
sentences.s125-s12: Whoever does not find refuge in my shadow will have his footing diminished.
sentences.s125-s13: Now I seek a bond with you; I will become the face of reconciliation.
sentences.s125-s14: Then your veiled one is a maiden; I have heard that at times she is fitting for me.
sentences.s125-s15: For she is of pure seed and of a pure body — praised in every city and every assembly.
sentences.s125-s16: If you find a son‑in‑law like the son of Qobad, consider it so: think that the sun has given him to you.
sentences.s125-s45: A certain host, adorned like paradise; you said that the surface of the earth was sown with tulips.
sentences.s125-s46: When King Kavus came near, his heart was calm, with beauty and with royal glory and rank.
sentences.s125-s47: Two smiling rubies, two stern narcissi; two columns of eyebrows like a silver pen.
sentences.s125-s48: Kavus looked and stood staring; he called to Sudabeh, invoking the name of God.
sentences.s125-s25: Thus he said to the sweet‑spoken man that this desire has neither head nor foundation.
sentences.s125-s26: He still demands of me two honorable things, which three others likewise do not know.
sentences.s125-s27: I took poor consolation from the desire; I had prepared my heart for a son.
sentences.s125-s28: From then on my life did not remain to me, and if the Shah of Iran were to seize it...
sentences.s125-s41: He set up one haftesālār (a commander of seven) over Hamāvarān — he carried out that task together with the chiefs.
sentences.s125-s42: Then Khosrow, weary of heart, brought (with him) three hundred and forty attendants.
sentences.s125-s43: A thousand mares and horses and a thousand camels; from brocade and dinars they made a load.
sentences.s125-s44: A dwelling adorned like the new moon; afterwards he wished for the inner chambers, both back and front.
sentences.s125-s21: He kept saying, 'Although he is a king, a sovereign, victorious and a ruler.'
sentences.s125-s22: For me, in the world there is this one daughter who is dearer than my sweet life.
sentences.s125-s23: If I treat the envoy coldly and humiliate him, I will have no followers or resources for battle.
sentences.s125-s24: It is better that I also turn a blind eye to this sorrow and let anger lie asleep upon my heart.
sentences.s125-s33: He continually wishes that, leaving me unsatisfied, she take away my heart, my sleep, and my peace.
sentences.s125-s34: What do you say now? What is your desire? What is your deliberate counsel about this matter?
sentences.s125-s35: Sudabeh said to him: 'There is no remedy for this; today there is no one a better mourner than he.'
sentences.s125-s36: Whoever was king of the world would, over the land, always desire (something) from guests.
sentences.s125-s29: I now leave whatever he wishes to do; I will not bend my head to his counsel and command.
sentences.s125-s30: He became sorrowful and summoned Sudabeh before him; he then addressed her about Kavus.
sentences.s125-s31: He told her that he is a distinguished, proud chief who, surpassing the moon and the best, is self‑sufficient.
sentences.s125-s32: A flattering envoy has arrived; a certain letter he holds in his hand.
sentences.s125-s37: From the bond with him there arose cause for gloom; no one regarded joy as anything but sorrow.
sentences.s125-s38: The leader (commander) of Hamavaran realized that that matter did not seem burdensome to Sudabeh.
sentences.s125-s39: He summoned the king's messenger into his presence, and from those he seated his nobles in the foremost places.
sentences.s125-s40: They concluded a compact according to their custom, in such a way that, at that time, their religion/law remained their own.
